lay_explanation:
Prolactin is a hormone produced by the pituitary gland. It is best known for its role in milk production during breastfeeding, but it also affects reproductive function.

When prolactin levels are elevated outside of pregnancy or breastfeeding - a condition called hyperprolactinemia - it can suppress ovulation. The mechanism is similar to why breastfeeding naturally reduces fertility: elevated prolactin signals the brain to reduce GnRH pulses, which in turn lowers FSH and LH production. The result can be irregular or absent menstrual cycles, reduced estrogen levels, and difficulty conceiving.

Prolactin levels may be checked as part of a fertility evaluation, particularly when menstrual irregularities are present.

Common Causes of Elevated Prolactin
Prolactinoma - a benign (non-cancerous) pituitary tumor that produces excess prolactin. This is the most common cause.

Medications - certain drugs, including some antidepressants, antipsychotics, and anti-nausea medications, can raise prolactin levels.

Thyroid dysfunction - an underactive thyroid can contribute to elevated prolactin.

Treatment
Prolactin-related fertility concerns are among the most treatable causes of ovulatory dysfunction. Medications called dopamine agonists (such as cabergoline or bromocriptine) effectively lower prolactin levels and restore normal ovulation in most cases. For prolactinomas, these medications often shrink the tumor as well. Once prolactin levels normalize, fertility typically returns.

Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) is one of the most common hormonal conditions affecting individuals of reproductive age. It is a leading cause of ovulatory infertility - meaning it disrupts the regular release of eggs from the ovaries.

PCOS is typically characterized by some combination of:

Irregular or absent menstrual cycles - due to infrequent or absent ovulation.

Elevated androgen levels - which can cause symptoms such as excess hair growth, acne, or hair thinning.

Polycystic-appearing ovaries on ultrasound - the ovaries may contain many small follicles that have not matured to the point of ovulation.

Not all individuals with PCOS have all three features. The condition exists on a spectrum, and symptoms vary widely.

PCOS and Fertility
The primary fertility impact of PCOS is irregular ovulation. Without regular ovulation, the timing of conception becomes unpredictable or impossible. However, with appropriate treatment, most individuals with PCOS can achieve pregnancy.

Treatment approaches are typically stepped:

Lifestyle modifications - even modest weight loss can restore ovulation in some cases and improve the response to other treatments.

Ovulation induction - medications such as letrozole or clomiphene citrate are used to stimulate the ovaries to develop and release an egg.

IVF - when ovulation induction is not successful, IVF is an effective option. Individuals with PCOS tend to respond well to stimulation medications, though the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) is higher. Modern protocols using GnRH antagonists, careful dosing, and freeze-all strategies help manage this risk.

PCOS is also associated with insulin resistance in many cases, which can contribute to weight gain and increase the risk of gestational diabetes during pregnancy. Addressing insulin resistance through lifestyle changes or medication (such as metformin) can improve both general health and fertility outcomes.

Advanced Maternal Age (AMA) is a clinical term used when the person providing eggs is 35 or older. This threshold reflects well-documented changes in both the number and chromosomal quality of eggs that occur with age.

Fertility begins to decline gradually around age 35, with a more noticeable drop after 37. This decline is driven primarily by two factors: a decreasing number of remaining eggs (ovarian reserve) and an increasing rate of chromosomal abnormalities in the eggs that remain. As the rate of aneuploidy (chromosomal errors) rises, fewer embryos are chromosomally normal, which affects both the chance of pregnancy per cycle and the risk of miscarriage.

IVF and AMA
While IVF success rates are lower with advancing age, several strategies help optimize outcomes:

Personalized stimulation protocols - medication dosing is adjusted based on ovarian reserve markers to maximize the number of eggs retrieved.

PGT-A (Preimplantation Genetic Testing for Aneuploidy) - testing embryos for chromosomal normality before transfer. This is particularly relevant after age 35, as it can identify the embryos most likely to result in a healthy pregnancy and reduce the risk of miscarriage. When a chromosomally normal embryo is transferred, pregnancy rates are comparable across age groups.

Freeze-all with single embryo transfer - freezing all embryos and transferring one at a time reduces the risk of complications while maintaining good success rates.

Egg freezing for fertility preservation - for individuals who wish to delay pregnancy, freezing eggs at a younger age preserves the chromosomal quality of those eggs for future use.

AMA presents real challenges, but individualized treatment planning based on ovarian reserve, response to stimulation, and embryo quality allows many individuals over 35 to achieve healthy pregnancies.

Anovulation means the ovaries are not releasing eggs during the menstrual cycle. Without ovulation, natural conception is not possible because there is no egg available to be fertilized. Menstrual periods may still occur - though often irregularly - even without ovulation, which is why the condition can go unrecognized.

Think of ovulation as a process that requires a specific sequence of hormonal signals. If any part of that sequence is disrupted, the follicle may not mature fully or may fail to release the egg.

Common Causes
The most common cause is PCOS (Polycystic Ovary Syndrome), where hormonal imbalances prevent eggs from maturing properly. Other causes include thyroid disorders, elevated prolactin levels (from pituitary issues), significant weight changes (either gain or loss), excessive exercise, high levels of stress, and the transition toward menopause. Temporary anovulation can also occur after discontinuing hormonal contraception.

Diagnosis
Anovulation is typically identified through blood tests (checking FSH, LH, thyroid hormones, and prolactin) and ultrasound monitoring. Identifying the underlying cause is important because it determines the most appropriate treatment.

Treatment
Anovulation is one of the most treatable causes of infertility. Treatment is directed at the underlying cause - for example, thyroid medication for thyroid disorders, or dopamine agonists for elevated prolactin. When the cause is PCOS or unexplained, ovulation induction medications such as letrozole or clomiphene citrate are typically the first step. If ovulation induction alone is not successful, IVF is an effective next option.

Diminished Ovarian Reserve (DOR) means the ovaries have fewer eggs remaining than expected for the individual's age. Think of the egg supply as a resource that was established before birth and decreases steadily over time - DOR means that resource has declined more than would typically be expected at a given age.

Diagnosis
DOR is diagnosed based on a combination of markers:

AMH (Anti-Mullerian Hormone) - a blood test that reflects the pool of developing follicles. A lower-than-expected AMH level for age suggests reduced reserve.

AFC (Antral Follicle Count) - the number of small follicles visible on ultrasound at the start of a cycle. A low count supports the diagnosis.

FSH - elevated day 3 FSH can be an additional indicator.

These markers predict how the ovaries are likely to respond to stimulation during IVF. They do not determine whether pregnancy is possible - they indicate how many eggs may be available to work with.

What DOR Means for Treatment
Individuals with DOR typically produce fewer eggs during IVF stimulation. However, when embryos are successfully created and transferred, pregnancy rates can be comparable to those without DOR. The challenge is in the numbers - fewer eggs means fewer embryos to select from, which may reduce the chance of having a chromosomally normal embryo available for transfer.

Treatment approaches may include adjusted stimulation protocols (different medications or dosing strategies), multiple retrieval cycles to accumulate embryos, or consideration of donor eggs when other approaches have not been successful.

DOR is a common finding in fertility evaluations and does not mean pregnancy is not achievable. Treatment planning focuses on making the most of the eggs that are available.

Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI) is a condition in which the ovaries lose their normal function before age 40. It is characterized by irregular or absent menstrual cycles and reduced production of reproductive hormones, particularly estrogen.

Unlike natural menopause, POI can be intermittent - the ovaries may occasionally produce hormones and release eggs unpredictably, which means spontaneous pregnancy, while uncommon, is not impossible.

Diagnosis
Current guidelines require elevated FSH levels along with several months of menstrual irregularity to confirm the diagnosis. Genetic testing, particularly for the Fragile X premutation, is recommended for all individuals diagnosed with POI, as it is found in a meaningful proportion of cases and has implications for future children.

Causes
In most cases, the cause of POI is not identified. Known causes include autoimmune conditions, genetic factors (such as Turner syndrome or Fragile X), chemotherapy or radiation treatment, and surgical removal of ovarian tissue.

Fertility Options
For individuals who wish to conceive, the options depend on the degree of remaining ovarian function:

IVF with own eggs - may be attempted if there is evidence of residual ovarian activity, though response to stimulation is often limited.

Donor egg IVF - for most individuals with POI, this provides the most reliable path to pregnancy, with high cumulative success rates over multiple cycles.

POI also has health implications beyond fertility - reduced estrogen levels affect bone density, cardiovascular health, and overall well-being. Hormone replacement therapy is typically recommended until the average age of natural menopause to address these concerns.

Endometriosis is a condition in which tissue similar to the lining of the uterus (the endometrium) grows outside the uterus - most commonly on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic surfaces. This tissue responds to hormonal changes during the menstrual cycle, causing chronic inflammation, pain, and the formation of scar tissue (adhesions).

Think of it as the uterine lining appearing where it does not belong. Because the tissue cannot shed and leave the body the way the normal endometrial lining does during menstruation, it triggers an ongoing inflammatory response.

How Endometriosis Affects Fertility
Endometriosis can reduce fertility through several mechanisms:

Ovarian cysts (endometriomas) - sometimes called "chocolate cysts" because they contain old blood. These can damage surrounding ovarian tissue and reduce egg supply. Surgical removal of endometriomas can further reduce ovarian reserve, which is why the decision to operate is made carefully.

Inflammation - the chronic inflammatory environment in the pelvis can affect egg quality, sperm function, and embryo implantation.

Adhesions - scar tissue can distort pelvic anatomy, block fallopian tubes, or interfere with egg pickup by the fimbriae.

Symptoms
Common symptoms include pelvic pain (especially during menstruation), pain during intercourse, heavy or irregular periods, and difficulty conceiving. However, some individuals have no symptoms and are only diagnosed during a fertility evaluation.

Diagnosis and Treatment
Endometriosis can be identified through advanced ultrasound or MRI, and in some cases through laparoscopy (a surgical procedure). Treatment depends on the individual's symptoms and goals:

Pain management - hormonal therapy can suppress the growth of endometrial tissue and reduce symptoms.

Surgery - may be recommended for large endometriomas or to remove adhesions, though the impact on ovarian reserve must be weighed.

IVF - for fertility purposes, IVF can bypass many of the barriers that endometriosis creates. While the condition may affect the number of eggs retrieved, the quality of those eggs can still support successful treatment when an adequate number are obtained.

Tubal factor infertility occurs when damage or blockage in one or both fallopian tubes prevents the egg and sperm from meeting. Since fertilization normally takes place in the fallopian tube, any obstruction in this pathway can prevent natural conception.

Think of the fallopian tubes as a corridor - if the corridor is blocked, traffic cannot flow in either direction.

Common Causes
Pelvic infections - particularly from sexually transmitted infections such as chlamydia or gonorrhea, which can cause scarring even without noticeable symptoms at the time of infection.

Endometriosis - can cause adhesions and scar tissue that distort or block the tubes.

Previous ectopic pregnancy - a pregnancy that implants in the tube can cause damage requiring surgical intervention.

Prior pelvic or abdominal surgery - including appendectomy, which can result in adhesion formation near the tubes.

Many individuals with tubal damage have no symptoms, which is why tubal assessment (through HSG or saline infusion sonohysterography) is a routine part of the fertility evaluation.

Hydrosalpinx
When a blocked tube fills with fluid - a condition called hydrosalpinx - it can reduce IVF success rates because the fluid may drain into the uterus and interfere with implantation. In these cases, the affected tube may be surgically removed or sealed before proceeding with IVF.

Treatment
IVF effectively bypasses the fallopian tubes entirely, as eggs are retrieved directly from the ovaries and fertilized in the laboratory. For individuals with tubal factor infertility who have otherwise normal egg quality and ovarian reserve, IVF outcomes are generally favorable.

Uterine fibroids (leiomyomas) are non-cancerous growths that develop in or on the muscular wall of the uterus. They are common and vary widely in size - from very small to large enough to change the shape of the uterus. Many individuals with fibroids have no symptoms, while others experience heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pressure, or pain.

How Fibroids Affect Fertility
The impact of fibroids on fertility depends primarily on their location:

Submucosal fibroids - grow into the uterine cavity (the space where an embryo would implant). These have the greatest impact on fertility and implantation and are generally recommended for removal before fertility treatment.

Intramural fibroids - grow within the muscular wall. Their effect on fertility depends on their size and whether they distort the uterine cavity. Larger intramural fibroids that indent the cavity may warrant removal.

Subserosal fibroids - grow on the outer surface of the uterus. These typically do not affect fertility unless they are very large.

Diagnosis
Fibroids are identified through ultrasound, saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS), or MRI. The location and size of the fibroids determine whether treatment is needed before proceeding with fertility care.

Treatment
Options include surgical removal (myomectomy), which can be performed hysteroscopically for submucosal fibroids or through other approaches for larger or deeper fibroids. The decision to treat is based on fibroid location, size, symptoms, and the individual treatment plan. When fibroids that distort the cavity are removed, IVF outcomes are generally comparable to those without fibroids.

Thin endometrium refers to a uterine lining that does not reach adequate thickness for embryo implantation, despite hormone preparation. In general, the endometrium is considered thin when it measures below approximately 7mm on ultrasound at the time of expected transfer.

Think of it as soil that has not built up enough depth for a seed to take root. The endometrium needs sufficient thickness and structure to support embryo attachment and early development.

Causes
Thin endometrium can result from reduced blood flow to the uterus, hormonal factors, scarring from previous uterine procedures (such as D&C), chronic endometritis (infection of the lining), or effects of certain medications. In some cases, no clear cause is identified.

Why It Matters
A thin lining is associated with lower implantation and pregnancy rates during IVF. It is one of the more challenging fertility issues to address because the lining does not always respond predictably to treatment.

Treatment Approaches
Extended estrogen therapy - the most common first step, allowing more time for the lining to build.

Improved blood flow - strategies such as sildenafil (which dilates blood vessels), vitamin E, or low-dose aspirin may be used to improve uterine blood supply.

Treatment of underlying causes - if scarring or infection is contributing, hysteroscopic surgery or antibiotics may be recommended.

The response to treatment varies between individuals. When the lining remains persistently thin despite intervention, the fertility team may consider alternative strategies such as adjusting the transfer timing or exploring other options.

lay_explanation:
Adenomyosis is a condition in which tissue from the endometrium (the uterine lining) grows into the muscular wall of the uterus (the myometrium). This causes the uterus to become enlarged, and the displaced tissue continues to respond to hormonal changes during each menstrual cycle, leading to inflammation within the muscle wall.

Think of it as the lining extending into a layer where it does not belong, causing chronic irritation from the inside.

Symptoms
Common symptoms include heavy and prolonged menstrual bleeding, significant menstrual cramping, and pelvic pain. The severity of symptoms varies - some individuals are significantly affected, while others may not know they have the condition.

How Adenomyosis Affects Fertility
Adenomyosis is associated with reduced implantation rates, lower live birth rates during IVF, and an increased risk of miscarriage. The inflammation and structural changes within the uterine wall are thought to interfere with the ability of the endometrium to support embryo attachment and early pregnancy.

The type of adenomyosis may also matter: diffuse adenomyosis (spread throughout the uterus) tends to have a greater impact on fertility outcomes than focal adenomyosis (concentrated in one area).

Treatment
Treatment strategies for adenomyosis in the context of fertility may include hormonal suppression before embryo transfer, adjusted transfer protocols, or medical management to reduce inflammation. The approach is individualized based on the severity and type of adenomyosis, as well as the overall treatment plan.

Many individuals with adenomyosis do achieve successful pregnancies with appropriate management.
