lay_explanation:
Sperm cryopreservation is the process of freezing and storing sperm for future use. It is one of the most established forms of fertility preservation.

How It Works
A semen sample is collected and mixed with a protective solution called a cryoprotectant, which prevents ice crystal damage to the cells during freezing. The sample is then frozen using controlled methods and stored in liquid nitrogen at extremely low temperatures (-196 degrees Celsius). When stored properly, sperm can remain viable for many years.

When It Is Recommended
Sperm cryopreservation is commonly recommended in the following situations:

Before cancer treatment - chemotherapy and radiation can damage or destroy sperm production, sometimes permanently.

Before vasectomy - as a safeguard in case fertility is desired in the future.

Before gender-affirming hormone therapy or surgery - for individuals who wish to preserve the option of biological parenthood.

When fresh sample availability is uncertain - for individuals who may have difficulty producing a sample on the day of a procedure, or who live far from the clinic.

During fertility treatment - as a backup in case a fresh sample cannot be collected on the day of egg retrieval.

Using Frozen Sperm
When the sperm is needed, it is thawed using a controlled warming protocol. Depending on the quality of the thawed sperm, it can be used for IUI (if count and motility are adequate) or for IVF with ICSI (if the numbers are limited). ICSI requires only a single viable sperm per egg, making it possible to use even very small quantities of frozen sperm.

Embryo cryopreservation is the process of freezing and storing embryos created during an IVF cycle for future use. It allows additional transfer attempts from a single egg retrieval, without repeating the full IVF process.

How It Works
The standard method is vitrification - an ultra-rapid freezing technique that prevents ice crystal formation, which could damage the embryo's cells. Embryos are treated with cryoprotectant solutions and then flash-frozen in liquid nitrogen at -196 degrees Celsius. When needed, they are thawed using a precise warming protocol.

Vitrification achieves very high embryo survival rates and has largely replaced older slow-freezing methods.

When Embryos Are Frozen
Embryos are most commonly frozen at the blastocyst stage (Day 5 or 6), though cleavage-stage embryos (Day 2 or 3) can also be frozen. Common reasons for freezing include:

Extra embryos from a fresh cycle - embryos beyond what is transferred can be stored for future use.

Freeze-all strategy - when medical reasons (such as OHSS risk or elevated progesterone) make a fresh transfer inadvisable.

PGT - when genetic testing is performed, all embryos are frozen while awaiting results.

Embryo banking - accumulating embryos from multiple retrieval cycles before transfer.

Outcomes
Pregnancy rates with frozen embryo transfer (FET) are comparable to - and in some cases slightly better than - fresh transfer. Frozen embryos can be stored for many years without loss of quality.

An important note: re-freezing an embryo that has already been thawed and not transferred is generally not recommended, as it can reduce survival and success rates.
related_to: Fresh Embryo Transfer - Day Five (Blastocyst Stage)

lay_explanation:
Oocyte cryopreservation, commonly known as egg freezing, is a fertility preservation technique in which mature eggs are retrieved from the ovaries and frozen for future use.

The Process
The process begins with ovarian stimulation using injectable medications for approximately 10 to 14 days, similar to the first phase of IVF. When the follicles reach the target size, a trigger shot is given, and eggs are retrieved through a transvaginal ultrasound-guided procedure under sedation. The mature eggs are then rapidly frozen using vitrification and stored in liquid nitrogen.

When It Is Considered
Egg freezing may be pursued for a variety of reasons:

Elective fertility preservation - for individuals who wish to delay childbearing for personal or professional reasons while preserving eggs at their current quality.

Medical fertility preservation - before cancer treatment, autoimmune disease therapy, or other medical interventions that could affect ovarian function.

Before gender-affirming treatment - for individuals who wish to preserve the option of biological parenthood before starting hormone therapy or undergoing surgery.

Using Frozen Eggs
When the eggs are needed, they are thawed and fertilized using ICSI (since the freezing process hardens the outer shell, making conventional insemination less effective). The resulting embryos are cultured and transferred as in a standard IVF cycle.

Research shows no increased risk of birth defects in children born from frozen eggs compared to fresh eggs. The younger the eggs are at the time of freezing, the better the expected outcomes when they are eventually used - which is why age at the time of freezing is an important factor in the decision.

Ovarian tissue cryopreservation is a fertility preservation technique in which strips of ovarian cortex (the outer layer of the ovary, which contains thousands of immature eggs within follicles) are surgically removed, frozen, and stored for future use.

How It Works
The tissue is obtained through a laparoscopic (minimally invasive) surgical procedure. Thin strips of the ovarian cortex are carefully processed and frozen using controlled methods, then stored in liquid nitrogen.

When It Is Used
This technique is particularly valuable in situations where other preservation methods are not possible:

Prepubertal patients - children who have not yet reached puberty cannot undergo egg freezing because their eggs are not mature enough to be retrieved. Ovarian tissue cryopreservation is currently the only fertility preservation option for this group.

Urgent cancer treatment - when there is not enough time to complete an egg freezing cycle before treatment must begin.

Individuals who cannot take hormonal stimulation - for example, those with hormone-sensitive cancers.

Future Use
When fertility is desired, the stored tissue can be transplanted back into the body (see Ovarian Tissue Transplantation topic). The transplanted tissue can restore both hormone production and the potential for natural conception or egg retrieval for IVF.

This technique was previously considered experimental but is now an established option at specialized centers. It offers the unique advantage of preserving a large number of immature eggs in a single procedure, without requiring hormonal stimulation.

Ovarian tissue transplantation is the procedure in which previously frozen ovarian tissue is surgically placed back into the body to restore ovarian function - including hormone production and, in some cases, fertility.

Approaches
There are two main approaches:

Orthotopic transplantation - the tissue is placed back near the remaining ovary or at the original ovarian site. This approach allows the possibility of natural conception, as eggs released from the transplanted tissue may be picked up by the fallopian tube.

Heterotopic transplantation - the tissue is placed at an alternative site, such as under the skin of the abdomen or forearm. This is used primarily to restore hormone production. If eggs mature in the transplanted tissue, they would need to be retrieved for IVF.

Outcomes
The transplanted tissue can begin producing hormones within weeks to months, restoring menstrual cycles and reducing the symptoms of hormone deficiency. Live births have been reported from ovarian tissue transplantation, and the technology continues to be refined.

Considerations
The longevity of the transplanted tissue varies - it may function for several years before the follicle supply is exhausted. For individuals whose original ovarian tissue was removed due to cancer, there is a theoretical concern about reintroducing cancer cells with the transplant, though this risk is considered low and screening methods continue to improve.

Ovarian tissue transplantation is performed at specialized centers and is an evolving area of reproductive medicine.

Testicular tissue cryopreservation involves surgically removing small pieces of testicular tissue and freezing them to preserve future fertility potential. It is currently the only fertility preservation option available for prepubertal individuals who cannot yet produce a semen sample.

How It Works
A minor surgical procedure is performed under general anesthesia to remove small tissue samples from one or both testes through tiny incisions. The tissue contains immature sperm precursor cells (spermatogonia) and supporting cells that have the potential to develop into mature sperm in the future. The tissue is processed and frozen using controlled techniques, then stored in liquid nitrogen indefinitely.

When It Is Used
The primary indication is for prepubertal patients facing treatments that could damage fertility - such as chemotherapy, radiation, or other gonadotoxic therapies - who cannot undergo sperm banking because they have not yet begun producing sperm.

Current Status
This technique is still considered experimental because the methods to mature the preserved tissue into functional sperm for use in reproduction are still under development. Research in animal models has shown promising results, including successful births from transplanted testicular tissue in non-human primates, which supports the possibility of future clinical applications.

Despite its experimental status, testicular tissue cryopreservation is offered at specialized centers because it is the only option for preserving fertility potential in this patient group. The procedure is generally safe with minimal complications.

Families are counseled about the experimental nature of the technique and the uncertainty about whether the tissue will be usable in the future, while acknowledging that it represents the best available option for preserving future reproductive options.

Prepubertal fertility preservation refers to the strategies used to protect the future reproductive potential of children who have not yet reached puberty but who require medical treatments that could damage their fertility - most commonly chemotherapy, radiation, or other gonadotoxic therapies.

As survival rates for childhood cancers have improved significantly, the long-term quality of life of survivors - including the ability to have biological children - has become an increasingly important consideration.

Options by Anatomy

For individuals with ovaries: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation is the primary option and is now considered an established (no longer experimental) technique. It involves surgically removing a portion of the ovarian cortex, which contains thousands of immature eggs, and freezing it for future transplantation. When the tissue is later transplanted back, it can restore both hormone production and the possibility of natural conception.

For individuals with testes: Testicular tissue cryopreservation is the only available option. It remains experimental, as methods to mature the preserved tissue into functional sperm are still under development. However, it is offered because no alternative exists for this patient group.

Timing and Decision-Making
The decision to pursue fertility preservation must be made before treatment begins, as fertility damage can occur after even a single cycle of certain chemotherapy drugs. This creates urgency in a context where families are already processing a serious diagnosis.

Counseling involves the patient (to the extent appropriate for their age and understanding), parents or guardians, the oncology team, and the fertility team. Ethical considerations include the child's ability to consent, the experimental nature of some techniques, and long-term decision-making about stored tissue.
related_to: Before Chemotherapy or Radiation for Non-Cancer Conditions

lay_explanation:
Banked (frozen) sperm can be thawed and used for fertility treatment when needed. The type of treatment depends on the quality of the sperm after thawing.

Treatment Options
If post-thaw sperm quality is adequate (sufficient count and motility), the sperm can be used for intrauterine insemination (IUI).

If post-thaw quality is reduced, IVF with ICSI is used. ICSI requires only a single viable sperm per egg, so even samples with low post-thaw counts can be effective.

Practical Advantages
Using banked sperm offers several benefits: it removes the pressure of producing a fresh sample on the day of a procedure, it provides a backup if collection is difficult on a treatment day, and it preserves fertility for individuals who banked before medical treatments (such as chemotherapy or hormone therapy) or before vasectomy.

Research consistently shows that outcomes using frozen sperm are comparable to those using fresh samples, provided the sperm are processed and used with the appropriate technique. Patients can be reassured that banking sperm does not compromise their chances of achieving pregnancy.

Certain medications used to treat autoimmune conditions - such as rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, inflammatory bowel disease, and other immune-mediated diseases - can damage reproductive cells and potentially cause temporary or permanent infertility. Fertility preservation before starting these treatments is an important consideration for individuals of reproductive age who may wish to have biological children in the future.

High-Risk Medications
Cyclophosphamide is among the most concerning, as it can cause significant damage to ovarian function, particularly in individuals over 30. Other medications with known reproductive risks include methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, and certain biologic therapies. The degree of risk depends on the specific drug, the dose, and the duration of treatment.

Preservation Options
For individuals with ovaries: egg or embryo freezing before treatment begins. In some cases, ovarian tissue cryopreservation may be an option.

For individuals with testes: sperm banking before treatment, particularly before cyclophosphamide or high-dose methotrexate.

Timing
The timing of fertility preservation is critical. Damage to reproductive cells can occur quickly after starting immunosuppressive treatment and may be irreversible. Ideally, fertility preservation is planned before treatment begins, though emergency protocols exist for situations where treatment is urgent.

Coordination between the treating specialist (rheumatologist, gastroenterologist, etc.) and the fertility team is essential to balance the urgency of disease treatment with the opportunity to preserve fertility. Many individuals with autoimmune diseases successfully preserve their fertility and later have healthy pregnancies with appropriate planning.
related_to: Before Chemotherapy or Radiation for Non-Cancer Conditions

lay_explanation:
Oncofertility is a field that addresses fertility preservation for individuals diagnosed with cancer. Cancer treatments - including chemotherapy, radiation, and certain surgeries - can damage or destroy reproductive cells, potentially leading to temporary or permanent infertility.

The key to successful fertility preservation is early intervention. Ideally, a fertility consultation and preservation procedures are completed before cancer treatment begins, as damage to eggs and sperm can happen quickly and may be irreversible.

Preservation Options

For individuals with ovaries:
Egg freezing or embryo freezing - the most established options. When time allows, ovarian stimulation and egg retrieval can often be completed within approximately two weeks.

Ovarian tissue cryopreservation - an option when there is not enough time for an egg freezing cycle, or for prepubertal patients who cannot undergo egg retrieval. This is now considered an established technique.

For individuals with testes:
Sperm banking - the standard approach, which can often be completed quickly before treatment begins.

Testicular tissue cryopreservation - for prepubertal patients who cannot produce a semen sample. This remains experimental.

Timing and Coordination
Cancer treatment timelines are often urgent, and fertility preservation must be carefully coordinated with the oncology team. Specialized oncofertility programs and navigators exist at many cancer centers to ensure patients receive timely information and access to preservation options.

Many cancer survivors live long and healthy lives after treatment. Preserving the option of biological parenthood before treatment allows individuals to make reproductive decisions in the future without being limited by the effects of their cancer therapy.

topic: Before Gender-Affirming Hormone Therapy or Surgery
lay_explanation:
Gender-affirming hormone therapy and certain gender-affirming surgeries can affect fertility. For transgender and gender-diverse individuals who may wish to have biological children in the future, fertility preservation before starting these treatments is an important option to discuss.

How Hormone Therapy Affects Fertility

Testosterone therapy (for transmasculine individuals) can suppress ovulation and cause changes in the ovaries. These effects may be reversible after stopping testosterone, but the degree and timeline of recovery can vary.

Estrogen therapy (for transfeminine individuals) can lead to reduced sperm production and testicular changes. Some degree of sperm production may persist during early treatment, but prolonged therapy can significantly impair or stop production.

Gender-affirming surgeries that involve removal of the ovaries (oophorectomy) or testes (orchiectomy) result in permanent loss of natural gamete production.

Preservation Options

For individuals with ovaries: egg freezing before starting testosterone therapy. Research has shown successful outcomes even after a period of testosterone use followed by cessation, though fertility treatments in this context may require sensitivity to gender dysphoria.

For individuals with testes: sperm banking before starting estrogen therapy. This can often be completed quickly and with minimal medical involvement.

For prepubertal individuals: ovarian or testicular tissue cryopreservation may be considered, though testicular tissue cryopreservation remains experimental.

Important Considerations
These conversations should ideally occur early in the transition planning process, before hormone therapy begins, as changes can start quickly. However, fertility preservation should be offered as an option - not a requirement - and the individual's autonomy and comfort should guide the process.

Healthcare providers increasingly recognize the importance of discussing fertility preservation with all transgender individuals in a supportive, affirming clinical environment that minimizes dysphoria while providing access to reproductive options.

Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) is an autoimmune condition in which the body produces antibodies that interfere with normal blood clotting. It is one of the identified and treatable causes of recurrent pregnancy loss.

How APS Affects Pregnancy
The antibodies associated with APS can cause abnormal blood clot formation in the placenta, reducing blood flow to the developing pregnancy. This can result in recurrent miscarriage (particularly in the second trimester), stillbirth, preeclampsia (dangerously high blood pressure during pregnancy), and restricted fetal growth.

APS may also affect fertility itself by interfering with endometrial function and implantation, and it has been associated with reduced ovarian reserve in some studies.

Diagnosis
APS is diagnosed through blood tests that detect specific antibodies - anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, and anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I. Because these antibodies can be temporarily elevated, the diagnosis requires positive results on at least two separate blood tests taken 12 or more weeks apart, along with a clinical history of blood clots or pregnancy complications.

Treatment
The standard treatment during pregnancy is a combination of low-dose aspirin and heparin (a blood thinner), which significantly improves pregnancy outcomes. Treatment is typically continued throughout pregnancy and for several weeks after delivery.

With appropriate treatment and monitoring by a high-risk pregnancy specialist, the majority of individuals with APS can achieve successful pregnancies. Early diagnosis is key, which is why APS testing is part of the evaluation for recurrent pregnancy loss.

Recurrent Pregnancy Loss (RPL) can have many different causes, though in a significant proportion of cases, the exact cause is not identified despite thorough evaluation.

The major established categories include:

Chromosomal abnormalities in the embryo - the most common identified cause. Errors in chromosome number or structure can prevent normal development. The risk increases with the age of the egg provider.

Parental chromosomal rearrangements - a small percentage of couples with RPL carry balanced chromosome rearrangements that, while not affecting their own health, can produce embryos with unbalanced chromosomes.

Uterine structural abnormalities - conditions such as a uterine septum, fibroids that distort the cavity, or other anatomical variations can interfere with implantation or pregnancy maintenance.

Endocrine disorders - including thyroid dysfunction, uncontrolled diabetes, or insufficient progesterone production during the luteal phase.

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) - an autoimmune condition that increases blood clotting and can reduce placental blood flow. This is one of the most treatable causes of RPL.

Thrombophilias (blood clotting disorders) - inherited conditions that increase the tendency to form clots, though the evidence for their role in RPL is mixed.

Immune factors - changes in immune cell populations, including natural killer cells and regulatory T cells, are an active area of research, though the clinical significance is still being established.

Even when a cause is identified, most individuals and couples with RPL can achieve a successful pregnancy with appropriate treatment. Comprehensive evaluation helps identify treatable factors and guides the management plan for future pregnancies.

Early miscarriage refers to pregnancy loss that occurs in the first trimester, before approximately 12 to 13 weeks of gestation. It is the most common type of pregnancy loss.

Causes
The most frequent cause is a chromosomal abnormality in the embryo - a random error that occurs during early cell division and prevents normal development. Other contributing factors can include hormonal insufficiency (such as low progesterone), structural abnormalities of the uterus, blood clotting disorders, and advancing reproductive age.

It is important to understand that early miscarriage is almost never caused by anything the pregnant individual did or did not do. Physical activity, work, stress, and minor incidents do not cause miscarriage. Most early losses are the result of genetic factors beyond anyone's control.

Management Options
When an early miscarriage is confirmed, there are typically three management approaches:

Expectant management - allowing the process to complete naturally without intervention.

Medical management - using medication (such as misoprostol) to help the uterus complete the process.

Surgical management (D&C) - a procedure to remove the pregnancy tissue, which may be recommended when there is heavy bleeding, infection risk, or patient preference.

The choice depends on clinical circumstances, how far along the pregnancy was, and personal preference. The medical team will discuss the options and support the individual through the process.

Most individuals who experience a single early miscarriage go on to have a healthy pregnancy in the future.

Pregnancy loss can have a profound emotional impact - not only on the individual who was pregnant, but also on partners and family members. The grief that follows is real and valid, regardless of how early in the pregnancy the loss occurred.

Common Emotional Responses
Feelings of sadness, guilt, anger, isolation, and anxiety are all common after pregnancy loss. Some individuals experience symptoms of depression or post-traumatic stress. The emotional effects can be compounded when losses are recurrent, as the hope and fear cycle of each new pregnancy adds cumulative stress.

Partners may also be deeply affected but may grieve differently or feel uncertain about how to express their feelings, particularly if the focus of care is primarily on the individual who was pregnant.

The emotional impact can persist well beyond the immediate period of loss. For some, it may affect future pregnancies, relationships, and overall well-being for months or years.

Support
There is no single "right" way to grieve, and everyone processes loss differently. However, support can make a meaningful difference:

Professional counseling - with a therapist experienced in pregnancy loss or perinatal grief.

Support groups - connecting with others who have experienced similar losses can reduce feelings of isolation.

Healthcare team support - compassionate, patient-centered care from the medical team, including acknowledgment of the loss and clear communication about next steps.

Bereavement programs - offered by some hospitals and fertility clinics.

Mental health support is a legitimate and important part of care after pregnancy loss. Seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness, and individuals should feel empowered to ask for the support they need.

After recurrent pregnancy losses (typically defined as two or more), a comprehensive evaluation is recommended to identify any treatable underlying causes.

What the Evaluation Includes
The workup typically covers several areas:

Genetic testing of pregnancy tissue - when available, the tissue from the pregnancy loss can be analyzed for chromosomal abnormalities. Modern techniques such as chromosomal microarray analysis (CMA) can detect abnormalities that older methods might miss and work even with non-viable tissue.

Parental genetic testing - both partners may have chromosomal analysis (karyotype) performed to check for balanced rearrangements that could contribute to abnormal embryos.

Blood tests - to evaluate thyroid function, blood clotting factors (thrombophilia screening), antiphospholipid antibodies, prolactin levels, and other hormonal markers.

Uterine evaluation - imaging (such as ultrasound, saline infusion sonohysterography, or MRI) to assess the shape and structure of the uterine cavity and identify any abnormalities such as a septum, fibroids, or adhesions.

Sperm DNA fragmentation - testing of the sperm-providing partner's DNA integrity, as elevated sperm DNA damage has been associated with pregnancy loss in some studies.

Interpreting Results
Even with thorough testing, a specific cause is not identified in approximately half of recurrent pregnancy loss cases. When this happens, the condition is classified as unexplained RPL.

The goal of the workup is to identify any treatable factors. When a cause is found, targeted treatment can improve the chances of a successful pregnancy. When no cause is identified, supportive care and close monitoring during future pregnancies have been shown to improve outcomes.

Recurrent Pregnancy Loss (RPL) is defined as two or more pregnancy losses. It is a distressing experience, but it is important to know that the majority of individuals and couples with RPL do go on to have a successful pregnancy, with or without treatment.

Causes
RPL is a complex condition with multiple potential contributing factors. Chromosomal abnormalities in the embryo account for the largest proportion of identified causes. Other established factors include uterine structural abnormalities, endocrine disorders (thyroid dysfunction, diabetes), blood clotting disorders, and autoimmune conditions such as antiphospholipid syndrome.

Despite thorough evaluation, a specific cause is not identified in many cases, which is classified as unexplained RPL.

Evaluation
A comprehensive workup is recommended after two or more losses to identify any treatable causes. This typically includes genetic testing, blood work, uterine imaging, and in some cases, sperm DNA fragmentation testing of the partner. See the Miscarriage Workup & Testing topic for details.

Treatment
Treatment depends on the identified cause:

Progesterone supplementation - for luteal phase support.

Thyroid medication - when thyroid dysfunction is present.

Aspirin and heparin - for antiphospholipid syndrome.

Surgical correction - for certain uterine abnormalities such as a septum.

PGT-A during IVF - to select chromosomally normal embryos, which may reduce miscarriage risk.

For unexplained RPL, supportive care in a specialized clinic - including close monitoring, reassurance, and early pregnancy support - has been shown to improve outcomes.

Prognosis
Age and the number of previous losses are the strongest predictors of future outcome, but successful pregnancy is possible even for individuals with multiple prior losses. Each case is unique, and individualized care planning is essential.

The Endometrial Receptivity Array (ERA) is a genetic test that analyzes the uterine lining (endometrium) to determine whether it is in a receptive state at the time an embryo would be transferred. The goal is to identify the individual's "window of implantation" - the specific timeframe when the endometrium is most ready to support embryo attachment.

How It Works
The test requires an endometrial biopsy, which is a brief procedure performed in the clinic. A small sample of the lining is collected and analyzed for the activity of a panel of genes associated with receptivity. Based on the results, the transfer can be timed to the individual's specific window, which may differ from the standard timing.

When It May Be Considered
ERA testing is primarily discussed for individuals who have experienced repeated implantation failure - multiple transfers of good-quality or euploid embryos without achieving pregnancy. Research suggests that a portion of these individuals may have a displaced implantation window.

Current Evidence
The evidence on ERA's effectiveness is mixed. Some studies show benefit in specific populations (particularly those with repeated implantation failure), while large-scale randomized trials have not shown consistent improvement in live birth rates for the general IVF population.

Major fertility organizations have noted that the evidence does not currently support routine use of ERA for all IVF patients. It may have value for carefully selected individuals with multiple unexplained failed transfers, but this should be discussed in the context of the overall treatment plan.

The test adds cost, requires an additional procedure, and delays the transfer cycle. These factors should be weighed against the potential benefit in the context of the individual situation.

Uterine polyps (also called endometrial polyps) are small, typically non-cancerous growths that develop on the inner lining of the uterus (endometrium). They can range from a few millimeters to several centimeters in size.

How Polyps Affect Fertility
Polyps can interfere with conception and pregnancy through several mechanisms: they may physically obstruct the uterine cavity, interfere with embryo implantation by disrupting the endometrial surface, cause local inflammation, or alter the hormonal environment of the lining. Research shows that removing polyps can meaningfully improve pregnancy rates compared to leaving them untreated.

Diagnosis
Polyps are typically identified through transvaginal ultrasound or saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS), which provides a clearer view of the uterine cavity. They may also be found incidentally during a hysteroscopy.

Treatment
The standard treatment is hysteroscopic polypectomy - a minimally invasive outpatient procedure in which a small camera is inserted into the uterus and the polyp is removed under direct visualization. Recovery is typically quick, and fertility treatment can usually resume after one menstrual cycle.

When to Treat
For individuals pursuing natural conception or IUI, polyp removal is generally recommended, as the evidence supports improved pregnancy rates after treatment.

For individuals undergoing IVF, the decision is more nuanced. Removal is clearly beneficial when polyps are large, located near the tubal openings, or when there have been repeated implantation failures. For smaller, incidental polyps found during IVF workup, the benefit of removal is less certain and the decision is made on an individual basis.

The overall message is encouraging - uterine polyps are treatable, and removal is a straightforward procedure that can improve fertility outcomes.
